agent_output:
{
  "executive_summary": "This system exhibits five interconnected architectural anti-patterns that have compounded over 3 years: shared database coupling, synchronous notification fan-in, god endpoint, HTTP-based background jobs, and cross-service direct database reads. Left unaddressed, these create a fragile system where any change to one service risks cascading failures across the platform. The remediation plan is sequenced to address the highest-blast-radius issues first (shared database, notification bottleneck) before tackling lower-severity concerns.",

  "architecture": {
    "components": [
      { "name": "Order Service", "responsibility": "Order lifecycle management", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — shares database with Courier Service, tables read directly by Reporting Service" },
      { "name": "Courier Service", "responsibility": "Delivery assignment and tracking", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — shares database with Order Service, creating implicit coupling at the schema level" },
      { "name": "Notification Service", "responsibility": "Sending notifications across channels", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — synchronous fan-in from 5 callers makes it a single point of failure for the entire platform" },
      { "name": "Customer Service", "responsibility": "Customer data management", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — god endpoint exposes unbounded aggregate, no separation of read concerns" },
      { "name": "Payment Service", "responsibility": "Payment processing", "boundaries": "Partially intact, but synchronously coupled to Notification Service" },
      { "name": "Reporting Service", "responsibility": "Analytics and reporting", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — bypasses Order Service API and reads its database directly" },
      { "name": "Cron Job Runner", "responsibility": "Scheduling background work", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — uses HTTP endpoints as a job execution mechanism, conflating API concerns with batch processing" }
    ],
    "interfaces": [
      { "from": "Order Service", "to": "Courier Service", "contract": "Shared PostgreSQL tables with schema prefixes", "coupling_type": "database coupling (worst form — schema-level tight coupling)" },
      { "from": "Order Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous REST call", "coupling_type": "temporal coupling — caller blocks until notification is sent" },
      { "from": "Courier Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous REST call", "coupling_type": "temporal coupling" },
      { "from": "Payment Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous REST call", "coupling_type": "temporal coupling" },
      { "from": "Customer Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous REST call", "coupling_type": "temporal coupling" },
      { "from": "Reporting Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous REST call", "coupling_type": "temporal coupling" },
      { "from": "Reporting Service", "to": "Order Service DB", "contract": "Direct SQL reads against Order Service tables", "coupling_type": "database coupling — bypasses service boundary entirely" },
      { "from": "Cron Runner", "to": "Various Services", "contract": "HTTP GET/POST to /api/v1/jobs/* endpoints", "coupling_type": "protocol misuse — HTTP as job scheduler" },
      { "from": "Any Client", "to": "Customer Service", "contract": "GET /api/v1/everything", "coupling_type": "god endpoint — returns unbounded payload, impossible to evolve independently" }
    ],
    "data_flow": "Currently: Services share data via shared databases (Order↔Courier, Reporting→Order DB) and synchronous REST calls. Notifications flow synchronously from 5 services into a single Notification Service. Background jobs are triggered by external cron hitting REST endpoints. Customer data is served as a monolithic blob. Target: Each service owns its data exclusively; cross-service communication via async events and well-defined APIs; notifications via message queue; background jobs via dedicated job infrastructure; customer data via purpose-built endpoints.",
    "patterns_used": [
      { "pattern": "Shared Database (ANTI-PATTERN)", "rationale": "Likely started as a shortcut when Order and Courier were one service. Now prevents independent deployment, schema migration, and scaling." },
      { "pattern": "Synchronous Fan-In (ANTI-PATTERN)", "rationale": "Notification Service became a convenience endpoint. Five synchronous callers means its downtime cascades to the entire platform." },
      { "pattern": "God Endpoint (ANTI-PATTERN)", "rationale": "The /everything endpoint likely grew incrementally as clients needed more data. Now it over-fetches, cannot be cached efficiently, and couples all customer subdomains." },
      { "pattern": "API-as-Job-Runner (ANTI-PATTERN)", "rationale": "REST endpoints for background jobs conflate API concerns with batch processing. No retry semantics, no dead-letter handling, no backpressure, cron has no visibility into job success/failure." },
      { "pattern": "Direct Cross-Service DB Read (ANTI-PATTERN)", "rationale": "Reporting reading Order tables directly is a shared-database anti-pattern variant. Reporting now depends on Order's internal schema, preventing Order from evolving its data model." }
    ]
  },

  "domain_model": {
    "bounded_contexts": [
      { "name": "Order Management", "classification": "core", "aggregates": ["Order", "OrderLine", "Refund"] },
      { "name": "Courier & Delivery", "classification": "core", "aggregates": ["Delivery", "CourierAssignment", "Route"] },
      { "name": "Customer Management", "classification": "supporting", "aggregates": ["Customer", "Address", "Preferences"] },
      { "name": "Payment", "classification": "core", "aggregates": ["Payment", "PaymentMethod", "Refund"] },
      { "name": "Notification", "classification": "generic", "aggregates": ["NotificationTemplate", "NotificationLog"] },
      { "name": "Reporting & Analytics", "classification": "supporting", "aggregates": ["Report", "Dashboard"] }
    ],
    "context_map": [
      { "upstream": "Order Management", "downstream": "Courier & Delivery", "relationship": "Currently: Shared Kernel (shared DB). Target: Customer-Supplier via domain events (OrderPlaced, OrderCancelled)" },
      { "upstream": "Order Management", "downstream": "Reporting & Analytics", "relationship": "Currently: Direct DB access (no boundary). Target: Published Language via CDC or event stream" },
      { "upstream": "All Services", "downstream": "Notification", "relationship": "Currently: Synchronous conformist. Target: Anti-Corruption Layer with async message queue" },
      { "upstream": "Customer Management", "downstream": "Order Management", "relationship": "Customer-Supplier — Order queries Customer API for customer data" },
      { "upstream": "Payment", "downstream": "Order Management", "relationship": "Customer-Supplier via events (PaymentConfirmed, RefundProcessed)" }
    ]
  },

  "trade_offs": [
    {
      "decision": "Split shared database between Order and Courier services (Priority 1)",
      "gains": "Independent deployability, independent schema evolution, independent scaling, clear ownership boundaries",
      "sacrifices": "Requires data migration, temporary dual-write period, increased network calls for cross-service queries, eventual consistency instead of transactional consistency",
      "alternatives_considered": ["Shared database with strict schema ownership rules (rejected: too easy to violate, doesn't solve deployment coupling)", "Merge into single service (rejected: they have different scaling profiles and team ownership)"]
    },
    {
      "decision": "Move Notification Service to async message queue (Priority 2)",
      "gains": "Eliminates single point of failure, decouples sender from notification delivery, enables retry/DLQ, improves response times for all 5 calling services",
      "sacrifices": "Adds message broker infrastructure (RabbitMQ/SQS/etc.), notifications are no longer guaranteed-sent when API returns, slightly more complex debugging",
      "alternatives_considered": ["Circuit breaker on sync calls (rejected: treats symptom not cause, still blocks callers)", "Async HTTP with webhooks (rejected: reinvents message queue poorly)"]
    },
    {
      "decision": "Replace god endpoint with purpose-built endpoints (Priority 3)",
      "gains": "Efficient caching, independent evolution of sub-resources, reduced payload size, better API documentation",
      "sacrifices": "Clients must update to call multiple endpoints or use a BFF, migration period where both old and new endpoints coexist",
      "alternatives_considered": ["GraphQL (viable alternative but adds complexity; fine if team has GraphQL experience)", "Keep god endpoint with sparse fieldsets/query params (rejected: doesn't fix the coupling problem)"]
    },
    {
      "decision": "Replace HTTP-cron jobs with proper job infrastructure (Priority 4)",
      "gains": "Retry semantics, dead-letter queues, job visibility/monitoring, backpressure, no HTTP timeout issues on long jobs",
      "sacrifices": "New infrastructure component (Sidekiq, Bull, Celery, or managed service), migration effort",
      "alternatives_considered": ["Keep HTTP endpoints but add job tracking table (rejected: still misusing HTTP, no retry/DLQ)", "Kubernetes CronJobs running CLI commands (viable simpler alternative if on K8s)"]
    },
    {
      "decision": "Replace Reporting direct DB reads with CDC or event stream (Priority 5)",
      "gains": "Order Service can evolve schema freely, Reporting gets optimized read model, separation of OLTP and OLAP concerns",
      "sacrifices": "CDC infrastructure (Debezium/equivalent), slight data lag, Reporting must build its own materialized views",
      "alternatives_considered": ["Read replicas with views (partial fix: still schema-coupled but isolates load)", "Order Service exposes reporting-specific API (viable for low-volume, but won't scale for analytics workloads)"]
    }
  ],

  "flexibility_points": [
    { "area": "Notification delivery mechanism", "evolution_path": "Once async, can swap email/SMS/push providers, add new channels, implement batching and digest notifications without touching any caller" },
    { "area": "Customer API surface", "evolution_path": "Decomposed endpoints enable independent versioning — can evolve address format without touching payment methods API" },
    { "area": "Reporting data pipeline", "evolution_path": "CDC/event stream enables adding new analytics consumers (ML models, dashboards, data warehouse) without touching Order Service" },
    { "area": "Background job infrastructure", "evolution_path": "Proper job framework enables priority queues, rate limiting, and job chaining without architectural changes" }
  ],

  "rigidity_points": [
    { "area": "Order-Courier shared database", "reason": "Until split, neither service can independently migrate schemas, scale databases, or change database technology. This is the highest-cost rigidity in the system." },
    { "area": "Notification synchronous coupling", "reason": "Notification Service downtime or slowness directly impacts response times and availability of 5 services simultaneously. Cannot be mitigated without architectural change." },
    { "area": "Order Service internal schema", "reason": "Reporting's direct reads mean any Order schema change requires coordinating with the Reporting team. Shadow coupling that's invisible in service dependency graphs." }
  ],

  "risks": [
    { "risk": "Shared database migration causes data loss or inconsistency during the split", "severity": "critical", "mitigation": "Use Expand-Contract pattern: (1) add new tables in Courier's own DB, (2) dual-write to both, (3) backfill, (4) verify consistency, (5) cut over reads, (6) stop writes to old tables. Never do a big-bang migration." },
    { "risk": "Notification Service outage cascades to all 5 calling services simultaneously", "severity": "critical", "mitigation": "Immediate: add circuit breakers and timeouts to all 5 callers as a tactical fix. Strategic: migrate to async messaging (Priority 2)." },
    { "risk": "God endpoint returns sensitive payment data to clients that only need addresses", "severity": "high", "mitigation": "Audit what each caller actually uses. Decompose endpoint. Consider field-level authorization." },
    { "risk": "HTTP-based background jobs silently fail with no retry or alerting", "severity": "high", "mitigation": "Add job result tracking table immediately as tactical fix. Migrate to proper job infrastructure as strategic fix." },
    { "risk": "Reporting queries cause performance degradation on Order Service's production database", "severity": "high", "mitigation": "Immediate: point Reporting at a read replica. Strategic: CDC to Reporting's own data store." },
    { "risk": "Cron-triggered refund processing endpoint is publicly accessible", "severity": "high", "mitigation": "Verify these job endpoints require authentication. If they're on the public API surface, restrict to internal network immediately." },
    { "risk": "Database split introduces distributed transaction failures between Order and Courier", "severity": "medium", "mitigation": "Identify which operations truly need transactional consistency (likely few). Use saga pattern or eventual consistency with compensating actions for the rest." }
  ],

  "implementation_guidance": {
    "recommended_approach": "Remediate in 5 phases over 6-9 months, sequenced by blast radius. Phase 1 (Month 1-2): Tactical stabilization — add circuit breakers to Notification callers, point Reporting at read replica, audit job endpoint security. Phase 2 (Month 2-4): Split Order/Courier shared database using Expand-Contract pattern. Phase 3 (Month 3-5): Introduce message queue, migrate Notification to async (start with lowest-risk caller, expand). Phase 4 (Month 4-6): Decompose Customer /everything endpoint into /profile, /addresses, /payment-methods, /orders, /preferences. Phase 5 (Month 5-9): Replace HTTP-cron jobs with proper job infrastructure, implement CDC for Reporting. Phases 3-5 can overlap.",
    "files_to_create_or_modify": [
      "Infrastructure: Message broker (RabbitMQ/SQS) for async notifications",
      "Infrastructure: Separate PostgreSQL instance for Courier Service",
      "Infrastructure: Read replica for Order DB (tactical, pre-CDC)",
      "Infrastructure: Job processing framework (Sidekiq/Bull/Celery)",
      "Order Service: Publish domain events (OrderPlaced, OrderUpdated, OrderCancelled)",
      "Courier Service: Subscribe to Order events instead of reading Order tables",
      "Notification Service: Consume from message queue instead of exposing sync API",
      "All 5 callers: Replace synchronous Notification calls with queue publish",
      "Customer Service: New endpoints /profile, /addresses, /payment-methods, /orders, /preferences",
      "Customer Service: Deprecation wrapper on /everything that logs usage and warns callers",
      "Reporting Service: CDC consumer or event stream subscriber, own materialized tables",
      "All job endpoints: Migrate to background job framework, remove HTTP job endpoints"
    ],
    "patterns_to_follow": "Strangler Fig for all migrations — new behavior alongside old, cut over incrementally, remove old code only after verification. Expand-Contract for database splits. Anti-Corruption Layer between contexts during migration. Domain Events as the primary integration pattern between services post-migration.",
    "pitfalls_to_avoid": [
      "Big-bang database migration — always use Expand-Contract with dual-write verification",
      "Replacing sync Notification calls without adding circuit breakers first — you need the tactical fix before the strategic one",
      "Building a custom job framework — use an established library (Sidekiq, Bull, Celery)",
      "Trying to make all 5 Notification callers async simultaneously — migrate one at a time, starting with the lowest-risk caller",
      "Ignoring the security exposure of HTTP job endpoints — audit these immediately regardless of the migration timeline",
      "Deprecating /everything without knowing which clients use which fields — instrument and measure first",
      "Introducing an event bus without schema registry — events become a new form of coupling without versioned schemas"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [],

  "confidence": {
    "level": 82,
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "All five anti-patterns are well-documented and widely recognized — the diagnosis is unambiguous",
      "Remediation patterns (Expand-Contract, async messaging, Strangler Fig) are battle-tested",
      "Priority ordering by blast radius is sound — shared DB and notification fan-in are clearly the most dangerous"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Specific technology choices (RabbitMQ vs SQS, Debezium vs custom CDC) depend on existing infrastructure stack which is unknown",
      "Timeline estimates (6-9 months) depend on team size, testing infrastructure, and deployment maturity",
      "Whether Order-Courier truly need separate databases or could be merged into one service depends on team structure and scaling requirements"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Services are deployed independently (otherwise shared DB is less painful but the other issues remain)",
      "Team has infrastructure support for running a message broker",
      "The 5-service synchronous fan-in to Notification is causing observable availability issues, not just theoretical risk",
      "Reporting workloads are heavy enough that direct DB reads cause measurable impact on Order Service"
    ]
  }
}
